frameworks.name: concealed information test
frameworks.one_line: Probe-vs-control item paradigm indexing recognition-driven autonomic arousal.

frameworks.name: pupillometry of emotional arousal
frameworks.one_line: Pupil dilation as autonomic readout of arousal during deception.

frameworks.name: sensory modality comparison
frameworks.one_line: Auditory versus visual probe presentation as competing input channels.

core_question: Does the sensory modality of probe items modulate pupil-based detection of concealed information during deception?
key_assumptions: Deception about recognized probes elicits stronger autonomic arousal than control items.
Pupil dilation reflects this arousal differentially across modalities.
Self-selected guilt assignment yields comparable engagement across groups.
manipulations: Group assignment (guilty theft mission vs not-guilty legal mission).
key_findings: Guilty group showed larger and earlier pupil dilation peaks for probe items.
Visual modality produced stronger discrimination between probe and control responses than auditory.
interpretations: Pupillometry indexes a sensitive concealed-information signal during deception.
The strength of this signal depends on the sensory modality of the probe.
